description: Exhaustive benchmark evals for the Noir privacy-app skill. Covers fundamentals, architecture, app-core wiring, frontend proof integration, testing, and ops/tooling.

instructions.step_1: For each eval, send the prompt to the model in a fresh conversation with NO skill loaded.
instructions.step_2: Grade the response pass/fail using the rubric. Be strict - partial credit is a fail.
instructions.step_4: Repeat with the skill loaded (paste noir/SKILL.md into context first). Record in 'with_skill' column.
instructions.step_5: Compare. Baseline pass + skill pass = content may be redundant. Baseline fail + skill pass = content is valuable. Baseline fail + skill fail = skill still needs work.
instructions.step_6: If noir/SKILL.md changes or any rubric changes, clear stale scores and rerun the full suite.
instructions.step_7: Report three views for every run: full-suite total, critical-only subtotal (importance = critical), and pass counts by coverage_area.
instructions.step_8: Do not compare totals across benchmark revisions unless the eval set and rubrics are identical.
instructions.step_9: If an eval asks for a concrete command, import path, config snippet, formula, or interface shape, a vague conceptual answer is a fail.

status: RUN 2026-04-01 - 34 evals on gpt-5.4. Baseline: 9 PASS / 25 FAIL. With skill: 30 PASS / 4 FAIL. Critical-only: baseline 3/17, with skill 13/17.

reporting.full_total: Use all evals for the main exhaustive comparison.
reporting.critical_only: Also report the subtotal for evals where importance = critical.
reporting.coverage_area_breakdown: Also report pass counts grouped by coverage_area.

evals.claim: nargo compiles/executes; bb handles proving, verification-key generation, and proof verification
evals.prompt: Show me the step-by-step commands to compile a Noir circuit, generate a proof, and verify it. Start from a project that already has src/main.nr written.

pass_criteria: Uses 'nargo compile' for compilation
Uses 'nargo execute' (NOT 'nargo prove') for witness generation
Uses 'bb prove' (NOT 'nargo prove') for proof generation
Uses 'bb write_vk' for verification key generation
Uses 'bb verify' (NOT 'nargo verify') for verification
fail_signals: Uses 'nargo prove' or 'nargo verify'
Omits the nargo execute step
Uses snarkjs or a non-Barretenberg backend

evals.claim: Check installed versions first, then install nargo before bb so bbup can match the installed nargo version
evals.prompt: I'm on a fresh machine and not sure what's already installed. How do I install Noir and bb safely?

pass_criteria: Checks existing versions first, such as 'nargo --version && bb --version'
Uses noirup to install nargo
Uses bbup to install bb
Installs nargo before bb
Explains that bbup reads the installed nargo version and installs a compatible bb
fail_signals: Blindly reinstalls without checking existing versions first
Installs bb before nargo
Uses made-up installers or package managers as the primary path
Does not explain why the install order matters

evals.claim: The production build pipeline stops at circuit artifact, VK, and Solidity verifier; browser proving happens later with NoirJS
evals.prompt: My app generates proofs in the browser. What is the minimal build pipeline from Noir source to a deployable Solidity verifier?

pass_criteria: Uses 'nargo compile'
Uses 'nargo execute'
Uses 'bb write_vk --oracle_hash keccak'
Uses 'bb write_solidity_verifier'
Explains that 'bb prove' is optional local smoke testing rather than the production proving path
fail_signals: Treats 'bb prove' as a required production build step
Omits 'nargo execute' or VK generation
Omits '--oracle_hash keccak'
Treats browser proof generation as part of verifier generation

evals.claim: Generated HonkVerifier contracts can hit the 24KB EIP-170 limit; local and real deployment handling differ
evals.prompt: My generated HonkVerifier is too large to deploy. What should I do locally, and what should I do for real deployments?

pass_criteria: Mentions the 24KB EIP-170 contract size limit
Uses a local override such as 'anvil --code-size-limit 40960'
Mentions using a matching code-size-limit override in local Forge deployment/testing flow
Recommends enabling the Solidity optimizer for real deployments
Includes optimizer settings such as 'optimizer = true' and 'optimizer_runs = 200'
fail_signals: Says to deploy as-is with no size discussion
Only gives generic optimization advice with no contract-size-limit handling
Uses the local size-limit override as the real deployment answer
Never distinguishes local testing from real deployment

evals.claim: Use the external Poseidon dependency and 'poseidon::poseidon::bn254::hash_1/hash_2', not removed stdlib imports
evals.prompt: I'm building a ZK circuit in Noir that needs to hash values for a commitment scheme. Which hash function should I use and why? Show me the Noir code.

pass_criteria: Recommends Poseidon (not SHA256 or Keccak) for in-circuit hashing
Mentions circuit efficiency or gate-cost difference as the reason
Says Poseidon is no longer in the Noir standard library and must be added as an external dependency, or shows that dependency explicitly
Uses 'use poseidon::poseidon::bn254::hash_1' or 'use poseidon::poseidon::bn254::hash_2'
fail_signals: Recommends SHA256 or Keccak for in-circuit use
Uses 'std::hash::poseidon::bn254::*'
Uses Poseidon2 or Pedersen as if they were drop-in replacements for the circuit hash
No explanation for why Poseidon is preferred

evals.claim: Noir 1.0 changed public input syntax from 'pub name: Field' to 'name: pub Field'
evals.prompt: Write the Noir function signature for a circuit that takes a private secret, a private nullifier, and a public merkle_root. Just the fn main() line with the parameters.

pass_criteria: Uses current Noir 1.0 syntax: 'merkle_root: pub Field' (pub after the colon)
secret and nullifier are plain 'Field' (no pub keyword)
fail_signals: Uses old syntax: 'pub merkle_root: Field' (pub before the parameter name)
Marks secret or nullifier as public

evals.claim: compiler_version in Nargo.toml rejects beta strings like >=1.0.0-beta.3
evals.prompt: I'm setting up a new Noir project for Noir 1.0. What should I put in my Nargo.toml? Show me the full file.

pass_criteria: Does NOT include a compiler_version with a beta string like >=1.0.0-beta.3
Either omits compiler_version entirely or uses a stable version like >=0.36.0
Includes a [dependencies] section (or explains when to add one)
fail_signals: Sets compiler_version to a beta string (>=1.0.0-beta.3 or similar)
Uses a compiler_version format that would fail Nargo validation

evals.claim: Generated HonkVerifier.sol requires pragma solidity >=0.8.21 and EVM version cancun
evals.prompt: I generated a Solidity verifier from my Noir circuit using bb write_solidity_verifier, but forge build fails with errors. My foundry.toml has solc_version = '0.8.17'. What's wrong?

pass_criteria: Identifies that the generated verifier requires pragma solidity >=0.8.21
Says to update solc_version to 0.8.21 or higher (for example 0.8.27)
Mentions the cancun EVM version requirement (evm_version = 'cancun' in foundry.toml)
fail_signals: Suggests only generic Solidity compilation troubleshooting
Does not mention the specific pragma or EVM version requirements
Says 0.8.17 should work fine

evals.claim: Do not overbuild: use a simple private proof unless you actually need anonymous membership from a shared set
evals.prompt: I'm building a Noir app that proves a user's salary is over $100k without revealing the exact amount. Do I need a Merkle tree and a nullifier scheme?

pass_criteria: Chooses a simple private proof instead of a commitment-nullifier app
Explains that no Merkle tree or nullifier is needed because there is no shared anonymity set
Says commitment-nullifier is for anonymous action from a shared participant set
fail_signals: Introduces a Merkle tree or nullifier scheme for this use case
Claims every privacy app needs the commitment-nullifier pattern
Does not distinguish private proofs from anonymous membership proofs

evals.claim: Anonymous shared-set actions require the commitment-nullifier pattern rather than a simple private proof
evals.prompt: I'm building anonymous voting where registered users should vote once per poll. Can I just use a simple private proof?

pass_criteria: Chooses the commitment-nullifier pattern rather than a simple private proof
Explains that the Merkle tree is the shared anonymity set
Explains that a nullifier or external_nullifier-scoped nullifier is needed to prevent double voting
Makes the poll-scoped nature explicit (for example pollId or external_nullifier)
fail_signals: Chooses a simple proof with no Merkle tree for this use case
Omits the nullifier or anti-replay mechanism
Does not explain the shared-set anonymity requirement

evals.claim: A working privacy app requires a circuit, app contract, generated verifier, offchain tree mirror, and frontend prover with distinct responsibilities
evals.prompt: What are the minimum subsystems of a Noir anonymous membership app, and what does each one do?

pass_criteria: Names the circuit and explains it proves note knowledge plus Merkle membership
Names the onchain app contract and explains it accepts inserts, checks roots/nullifiers, and executes the action
Names the generated verifier as a distinct deployed contract or interface boundary
Names the offchain tree mirror and explains it rebuilds the tree from events and produces leafIndex/siblings/root
Names the frontend prover and explains it creates or persists notes, executes Noir, generates proofs, serializes calldata, and submits the action
fail_signals: Treats the circuit and contract as the whole system
Omits the offchain tree mirror
Omits the generated verifier as a separate subsystem
Does not distinguish responsibilities across layers

evals.claim: The circuit artifact, generated verifier, and frontend-consumed JSON should live in a stable explicit layout rather than ad hoc copying
evals.prompt: How should I organize Noir artifacts across circuits, contracts, and frontend in a monorepo?

pass_criteria: Gives a stable layout or equivalent for the compiled circuit artifact consumed by NoirJS
Places the generated verifier source in the contracts area
Places the frontend-consumed circuit JSON in a frontend-visible path such as public/
Warns against hand-copying artifacts ad hoc
fail_signals: Gives no artifact hand-off plan
Treats artifacts as one-off copied blobs with no stable location
Omits either the verifier source or the frontend-consumed circuit artifact

evals.claim: 'bb write_solidity_verifier' plus '--oracle_hash keccak' is required for an EVM-compatible verifier
evals.prompt: How do I generate a Solidity verifier contract from my compiled Noir circuit so I can verify proofs onchain?

pass_criteria: Uses 'bb write_vk' to generate a verification key
Includes '--oracle_hash keccak' on the write_vk command
Uses 'bb write_solidity_verifier'
Explains that the keccak oracle hash is required for EVM compatibility
fail_signals: Uses nargo to generate the verifier
Uses snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier
Uses 'bb contract'
Omits '--oracle_hash keccak'

evals.claim: The generated verifier is a standalone contract - deploy it separately, pass the address to the app contract via constructor
evals.prompt: I have my generated HonkVerifier.sol from the Noir toolchain. How do I wire it into my privacy app contract? Show me the Foundry deploy script.

pass_criteria: Deploys the verifier as a standalone contract first
Passes the deployed verifier address to the app contract (via constructor or setter)
The app contract stores the verifier as an immutable or state variable and calls it for proof verification
fail_signals: Imports the verifier and inherits from it instead of deploying separately
Hardcodes the verifier logic inside the app contract
Does not deploy the verifier at all

evals.claim: The generated verifier ABI is the source of truth; if the app wants a stable interface, it needs a dedicated adapter or exact ABI mirroring
evals.prompt: My generated HonkVerifier ABI doesn't match a generic 'verify(bytes, bytes32[])' interface. How should I integrate it into my app contract?

pass_criteria: Says the generated verifier ABI is the source of truth
Explains that the app should either mirror that ABI exactly or wrap it in a dedicated adapter contract
Avoids assuming every verifier exposes a generic 'verify(bytes, bytes32[])' signature
Treats the adapter or wrapper as an interface boundary rather than rewriting verifier logic
fail_signals: Assumes the verifier always exposes 'verify(bytes, bytes32[])'
Suggests guessing the verifier signature from memory
Inlines or rewrites the verifier logic instead of adapting to the generated ABI

evals.claim: A correct commitment-nullifier circuit needs proper inputs, Poseidon hashing, Merkle verification, and constrained indices
evals.prompt: Write a complete Noir circuit (main.nr) for an anonymous voting system. The circuit should prove: the voter knows a secret that corresponds to a registered commitment in a Merkle tree, and output a nullifier hash to prevent double voting. Include the Merkle proof verification.

pass_criteria: Defines 'fn main()' with private inputs for nullifier, secret, Merkle siblings/path, and position information
Marks public inputs using current Noir syntax ('name: pub Field')
Uses 'poseidon::poseidon::bn254::hash_1' or 'hash_2' from the external Poseidon dependency
Recomputes the commitment from private inputs
Verifies the Merkle proof against the public root
Computes and constrains the nullifier hash
Either uses '[u1; depth]' for path indices or otherwise constrains indices/bits to binary values
fail_signals: Uses Circom syntax ('signal', 'template', 'component')
Uses removed stdlib Poseidon imports, Pedersen, or made-up Field helpers
Skips assert constraints so witness values can float
Omits Merkle traversal logic

evals.claim: The recommended helper path for production Merkle verification is zk-kit.noir's binary_merkle_root with u1 indices
evals.prompt: Show me the recommended Noir code to verify a Poseidon Merkle proof using zk-kit.noir instead of writing the loop by hand.

pass_criteria: Adds or references the 'binary_merkle_root' dependency from zk-kit.noir
Imports 'binary_merkle_root::binary_merkle_root'
Uses 'use poseidon::poseidon::bn254::hash_2'
Calls 'binary_merkle_root' with the 5-argument shape: hasher, leaf, depth, indices, siblings
Uses '[u1; TREE_DEPTH]' or equivalent typed binary indices
fail_signals: Only writes a manual Merkle loop with no zk-kit.noir helper
Uses the wrong helper name or wrong argument order
Uses unconstrained Field indices with no binary enforcement
Uses removed stdlib Poseidon imports

evals.section: Production Hardening: Domain Separation and Action Binding
evals.claim: Action-scoped apps need domain separation and an action-specific nullifier binding such as external_nullifier or pollId
evals.prompt: I have an anonymous voting app with multiple polls. Show the Noir-side formula or code for computing the nullifier hash so a proof cannot be reused across polls, and explain which inputs are public vs private.

pass_criteria: Introduces an action-specific binding such as 'external_nullifier' or 'pollId'
Gives a concrete in-circuit formula or code shape, such as 'hash_2([external_nullifier, nullifier])'
Explains that the action-specific value is public and the nullifier remains private
Explains that this prevents replay across polls or actions
Mentions domain separation between commitment hashing and nullifier hashing
fail_signals: Uses only 'hash(nullifier)' with no action binding
Claims a nullifier alone prevents cross-poll replay
Says only 'include the poll ID somehow' with no formula or code shape
Binds the action only offchain or in app prose instead of in-circuit
Does not mention domain separation or action scoping

evals.claim: A working app must persist the privacy note and enough metadata to reconstruct proof inputs later
evals.prompt: What exactly must my frontend save when a user deposits a Noir privacy note so it can later generate a proof?

pass_criteria: Saves 'nullifier', 'secret', and 'commitment'
Saves chain/contract metadata and tree depth, or the equivalent proving context
Persists 'leafIndex' or explicitly says it must be recovered from the insert event
Says the tree/proof path is later reconstructed from events or an offchain tree mirror
Explains that losing the note means the user cannot later prove or act
fail_signals: Says to save only the commitment
Ignores 'leafIndex' and event replay entirely
Assumes the contract alone can reconstruct the full witness

evals.claim: The contract needs explicit insert events, nullifier storage, and an accepted-root policy the client can build against
evals.prompt: What state and events should my Solidity app contract expose for a Noir membership app?

pass_criteria: Includes used-nullifier storage/checks
Includes an insert event with commitment, leaf index, and resulting root
Makes the accepted-root policy explicit (recent root history, or current-root-only with an explicit warning)
Says the client rebuilds the tree from events
Says proofs are verified before performing side effects or state mutations
fail_signals: Only stores a single Merkle root with no policy discussion
Omits the insert event or leaf index
Omits nullifier storage/checks
Never explains how the client gets Merkle siblings
Does not say proof verification gates side effects

evals.claim: Use audited LeanIMT tooling plus a matching client mirror instead of hand-rolling the commitment tree
evals.prompt: Build a Noir ZK membership proof app on Ethereum. How do you store commitments onchain?

pass_criteria: Uses LeanIMT from '@zk-kit/lean-imt.sol' rather than a custom Merkle implementation
Mentions the PoseidonT3 dependency
Uses '@zk-kit/lean-imt' client-side to mirror the tree
Mentions generating Merkle siblings from the mirrored tree using 'generateProof(leafIndex)' or the equivalent leaf-indexed proof path
fail_signals: Implements a custom Merkle tree contract from scratch
Uses an unaudited or made-up library
Does not connect the onchain tree to an offchain mirror/proof generator
Omits PoseidonT3 or the client mirror entirely

evals.section: Hash Parity Across Circuit, Tree, and Contract
evals.claim: Circuit, offchain tree mirror, and onchain tree must use the same hash algorithm and input ordering
evals.prompt: How do I keep my Noir circuit, offchain Merkle tree, and onchain tree contract compatible?

pass_criteria: States that the same algorithm and input ordering must be used across every layer
Explicitly says Poseidon, Poseidon2, and Keccak are not interchangeable
Recommends leaf-hash and parent-hash parity tests with known inputs before building the full tree
fail_signals: Says any Poseidon variant is fine
Mixes Poseidon and Poseidon2 without warning
Does not recommend parity testing

evals.claim: Noir/Barretenberg verifiers work on mainstream EVM chains with BN254 precompiles, including zkSync ERA with caveats
evals.prompt: I want to deploy a Noir-based privacy app. Which EVM chains can I deploy the Solidity verifier to? Are there any chains where it won't work?

pass_criteria: Lists mainstream EVM chains as compatible (Ethereum, Optimism, Arbitrum, Base)
Identifies BN254 precompiles as the requirement
Treats zkSync ERA as compatible but notes caveats such as higher gas or smart-contract precompile implementation
Warns about Polygon zkEVM being shut down or otherwise not a target
fail_signals: Says all chains are compatible with no precompile discussion
Says zkSync ERA is incompatible
Says Polygon zkEVM is a normal supported target
Does not mention precompile requirements

evals.claim: ZK proofs hide the link between commitment and nullifier, but msg.sender is public. Same wallet committing and acting is trivially linkable.
evals.prompt: I built a Noir anonymous voting app. Users commit to a Merkle tree from their wallet, then later submit a ZK proof to vote from the same wallet. Is this actually anonymous?

pass_criteria: Identifies that msg.sender is public and links the committing wallet to the voting wallet
Explains that using the same wallet for both commit and act breaks the anonymity the ZK proof provides
Recommends using a different wallet for voting (burner wallet, relayer, or ERC-4337 paymaster)
fail_signals: Says the ZK proof makes the vote anonymous without qualification
Does not mention msg.sender or wallet linkability
Treats the app as fully private because the circuit hides the secret

evals.claim: A simple private proof flow should stay simple: no Merkle tree, no nullifier, but still include verifier generation, proof generation, serialization, and onchain verification
evals.prompt: Outline the minimal end-to-end flow for a simple Noir threshold-proof app that proves a private value exceeds a threshold and does NOT use an anonymity set.

pass_criteria: Does not introduce a Merkle tree or nullifier
Uses a circuit that proves the threshold condition over private data
Includes Solidity verifier generation with '--oracle_hash keccak'
Includes proof generation via NoirJS or backend proving
Includes proof/public-input serialization before onchain verification
fail_signals: Introduces the commitment-nullifier pattern for this use case
Stops at circuit code with no verifier/proof/onchain flow
Omits verifier generation or proof serialization

evals.claim: A correct answer must connect note creation, insert events, tree reconstruction, proof generation, serialization, and nullifier-based replay protection
evals.prompt: Outline the minimal end-to-end flow for a Noir anonymous membership app from commitment insertion to a successful 'act()' call.

pass_criteria: Generates and saves the note ('nullifier', 'secret', 'commitment')
Inserts the commitment onchain and captures 'leafIndex' / root from the insert event
Rebuilds the Merkle tree offchain and derives siblings for the stored leaf index
Runs Noir execution plus proof generation
Serializes the proof and public inputs correctly before contract submission
Submits the action and explains that the nullifier prevents replay or double action
fail_signals: Stops at circuit code or verifier deployment
Skips event-driven tree reconstruction
Omits proof/public-input serialization
Omits nullifier replay protection

evals.claim: Browser proving uses '@noir-lang/noir_js' plus '@aztec/bb.js' with Barretenberg and UltraHonkBackend
evals.prompt: Show me how to generate a Noir ZK proof in the browser using JavaScript/TypeScript. Include the imports, initialization, and proof generation flow.

pass_criteria: Imports from '@noir-lang/noir_js'
Imports 'Barretenberg' and 'UltraHonkBackend' from '@aztec/bb.js'
Creates a Barretenberg instance before backend initialization
Creates 'UltraHonkBackend' using the circuit bytecode plus the Barretenberg instance
Uses 'noir.execute(inputs)' to generate the witness
Uses 'backend.generateProof(witness)' to create the proof
Identifies the proof payload as proof bytes ('Uint8Array') plus public inputs
fail_signals: Imports from '@noir-lang/backend_barretenberg'
Uses 'UltraPlonkBackend' as the primary path
Uses 'noir.generateFinalProof()'
Uses snarkjs in the browser path
Skips Barretenberg initialization entirely

evals.claim: NoirJS uses WASM and requires specific Vite configuration: node polyfills plus esnext target
evals.prompt: I'm building a React app with Vite that generates Noir proofs in the browser. When I import @noir-lang/noir_js, I get WASM-related build errors. How do I configure Vite to work with NoirJS?

pass_criteria: Adds vite-plugin-node-polyfills (or equivalent node polyfills plugin)
Sets build target and/or optimizeDeps target to 'esnext' for top-level await support
Mentions that NoirJS uses WASM which requires these specific configurations
fail_signals: Gives only generic WASM advice without Noir-specific configuration
Does not mention node polyfills
Does not address top-level await or esnext target

evals.claim: Next.js and Scaffold-ETH 2 need asyncWebAssembly, client-only rendering, and circuit loading from public/ via fetch()
evals.prompt: I'm using Next.js or Scaffold-ETH 2 for browser-side Noir proving. What config and loading pattern do I need?

pass_criteria: Enables webpack asyncWebAssembly
Uses client-only rendering such as 'use client' and/or 'dynamic(..., { ssr: false })'
Loads the circuit JSON from 'public/' via 'fetch()'
Explains that SSR or cross-package JSON imports are the problem for this setup
fail_signals: Gives only generic Next.js advice
Does not mention asyncWebAssembly
Does not make the Noir component client-only
Imports the circuit artifact directly from another package with no warning

evals.claim: The proof boundary must normalize bytes and field values before calling Solidity, and the generated verifier ABI is the source of truth
evals.prompt: Show me how to safely send a NoirJS proof from the browser to Solidity.

pass_criteria: Converts 'Uint8Array' proof bytes to '0x...' hex before RPC submission
Normalizes public inputs to 32-byte hex / 'bytes32' values
Preserves the exact circuit/verifier public-input order
Says the generated verifier ABI is the source of truth
fail_signals: Passes raw 'Uint8Array' directly as if it were already RPC-safe
Assumes any verifier exposes a generic 'verify(bytes, bytes32[])' signature
Ignores public-input normalization or ordering
Uses 'Buffer' as the browser-side solution

evals.claim: If vote is 0 or 1, hash(0) and hash(1) are trivially brutable as public outputs - add a salt
evals.prompt: My anonymous voting circuit takes a private vote (0 or 1) and outputs hash(vote) as a public input so the contract can tally votes. A reviewer says there's a privacy issue. What is it?

pass_criteria: Identifies that hash(0) and hash(1) are only two possible values, so anyone can precompute both and determine the vote
Explains this is a small-domain brute force attack
Recommends adding a random salt or blinding factor to the hash input
fail_signals: Says the hash hides the vote sufficiently
Does not identify the brute-force risk for binary-domain values
Suggests the privacy issue is something else entirely

evals.claim: A real zk app test plan must include concrete negative-path tests across root handling, siblings, nullifiers, and serialization/order boundaries
evals.prompt: What negative tests should a Noir anonymous membership app have beyond the happy path?

pass_criteria: Includes reused nullifier failure
Includes wrong root failure
Includes wrong sibling ordering or wrong Merkle path failure
Includes stale-root failure or explicit root-policy failure
Includes mismatched public input order or serialization failure
fail_signals: Lists only happy-path tests
Gives only generic 'invalid proof should fail' with no concrete cases
Omits either nullifier, Merkle-path/root, or serialization-order failures

evals.claim: MockVerifier is for tests only; production deploy scripts and real verification tests must use the generated HonkVerifier
evals.prompt: In Foundry, can I use a MockVerifier in my deployment script for a Noir app?

pass_criteria: Says mock verifiers are test-only
Says deployment scripts and production wiring must use the real generated HonkVerifier
If tests are discussed, distinguishes mock-based unit tests from at least one real-verifier integration test
fail_signals: Says MockVerifier is fine for deployment or production-like wiring
Does not distinguish test-only use from real deployment
Never mentions the generated HonkVerifier

evals.claim: Agents that need live Noir docs beyond the skill should use noir-mcp-server
evals.prompt: If an agent needs live Noir docs and examples beyond this skill, what should it use and how should it set it up?

pass_criteria: Names 'noir-mcp-server'
Gives the setup command 'claude mcp add noir-mcp -- npx @critesjosh/noir-mcp-server@latest'
Mentions '/reload-plugins'
Describes that it indexes Noir compiler/docs/examples or community libraries
fail_signals: Only suggests generic web search or browsing docs manually
Does not mention noir-mcp-server
Omits setup entirely

historical_notes.note: Earlier benchmark iterations focused mostly on syntax/toolchain drift. This revision keeps the app-core evals and adds the remaining skill sections so the suite covers the whole skill rather than only the highest-value failures.
2026-03-31 scores were produced against the earlier 21-eval suite and are historical only. They are not directly comparable to the exhaustive 34-eval suite introduced on 2026-04-01.
The benchmark now tracks 'coverage_area' and 'importance' per eval so model comparisons can be reported as full-suite totals, critical-only subtotals, and coverage-area breakdowns.
The benchmark currently mirrors the import path taught by the skill ('poseidon::poseidon::bn254::*'). If the skill changes, update the benchmark and rerun the full suite in the same change.

scoring.baseline_pass_and_skill_pass: REDUNDANT - the base model already knows this. Consider trimming the skill if this remains true over time.
scoring.baseline_fail_and_skill_pass: VALUABLE - the skill is correcting a real failure mode.
scoring.baseline_fail_and_skill_fail: BROKEN - the skill discusses this topic but still does not fix the model behavior.
scoring.baseline_pass_and_skill_fail: REGRESSION - the skill made the model worse. Investigate immediately.

summary: 2026-04-01 exhaustive run on gpt-5.4, judged strictly with gpt-5.4: baseline 9/34 PASS, with skill 30/34 PASS. Critical-only: baseline 3/17, with skill 13/17. Coverage: fundamentals 2/7 -> 6/7; architecture 3/6 -> 6/6; app_core 2/12 -> 10/12; frontend 1/4 -> 3/4; ops 1/5 -> 5/5. Raw outputs: /tmp/noir-benchmark-gpt54-exhaustive-2026-04-01-postfix.
